SUMMARY:
Veterinary public health - Wikipedia Veterinary public health Conventionally veterinary public health as a topic covers the following areas: Veterinary public health concerns all aspects of food production chain from controlling epidemic diseases that may impact on agriculture, to ensuring slaughter is conducted safely and humanely, to informing the public on safe ways to store and cook hamburgers. Veterinary public health concerns the surveillance and control of zoonoses at many different levels be it via disease control programmes at farm level or wild animals or in the abattoir. *   _As of this edit, this article uses content from_"Introduction to VPH"_, which is licensed in a way that permits reuse under the Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 3.0 Unported License, but not under the GFDL. From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Component of public health that focuses on the application of veterinary science

[![Image 4](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/99/Question_book-new.svg/60px-Question_book-new.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg)This article **needs additional citations for [verification](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Special:EditPage/Veterinary_public_health "Special:EditPage/Veterinary public health") by [adding citations to reliable sources](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed.

_Find sources:_["Veterinary public health"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Veterinary+public+health%22)–[news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Veterinary+public+health%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1)**·**[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Veterinary+public+health%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks)**·**[books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Veterinary+public+health%22+-wikipedia)**·**[scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Veterinary+public+health%22)**·**[JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Veterinary+public+health%22&acc=on&wc=on)_(November 2013)_ _([Learn how and when to remove this message](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:Maintenance\_template\_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))_

**Veterinary public health** (**VPH**) is a component of [public health](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Public_health "Public health") that focuses on the application of [veterinary science](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Veterinary_medicine "Veterinary medicine") to protect and improve the physical, mental and social well-being of humans.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Veterinary_public_health#cite_note-1) In several countries activities related to VPH are organized by the [chief veterinary officer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chief_Veterinary_Officer "Chief Veterinary Officer").

Conventionally veterinary public health as a topic covers the following areas:

Food production and safety
--------------------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Veterinary_public_health&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Food production and safety")]

It is desirable to consider food production as a chain, with animals reared on the [farm](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Farm "Farm") (pre-harvest) then going for primary processing (harvest), secondary processing and distribution followed by final preparation (all post-harvest). This "farm to fork" concept can be easily described by considering a [beef](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beef "Beef") animal on a farm going to slaughter at the [abattoir](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Abattoir "Abattoir"), then the [hamburger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hamburger "Hamburger") production plant, then being distributed to a [supermarket](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Supermarket "Supermarket"). The hamburger is then sold, taken home, stored, cooked and eaten.

Veterinary public health concerns all aspects of food production chain from controlling [epidemic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epidemic "Epidemic") diseases that may impact on agriculture, to ensuring [slaughter](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Animal_slaughter "Animal slaughter") is conducted safely and humanely, to informing the public on safe ways to store and cook hamburgers.

Zoonosis control
----------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Veterinary_public_health&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Zoonosis control")]

A [zoonosis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zoonosis "Zoonosis") may be defined as any [disease](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Disease "Disease") and/or [infection](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Infection "Infection") which is naturally transmissible between animals and man.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Veterinary_public_health#cite_note-2)

They are a major public concern. Headlines on issues like [avian influenza](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Avian_influenza "Avian influenza"), [BSE](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bovine_spongiform_encephalopathy "Bovine spongiform encephalopathy") (mad cow disease) and [salmonella](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Salmonella "Salmonella") of eggs have dominated the UK newspaper headlines for the last thirty years.

The picture in developed and developing countries may be quite different as far as [zoonoses](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zoonoses "Zoonoses") are concerned. In developed countries the [consumer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Consumer "Consumer") has very little contact with the live animal, limiting transmission from live animals to the general public. In addition [food safety](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Food_safety "Food safety") is extremely regulated. Despite this [food borne disease](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Foodborne_illness "Foodborne illness") is still a big problem in developed countries. In the [EU](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/European_Union "European Union") in 2006, a total of 175,561 confirmed cases of [campylobacteriosis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Campylobacteriosis "Campylobacteriosis") were reported from 21 member states and reported cases will only represent a portion of the total.[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Veterinary_public_health#cite_note-3)

Veterinary public health concerns the surveillance and control of zoonoses at many different levels be it via disease control programmes at farm level or [wild animals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wild_animal "Wild animal") or in the abattoir.

Environmental contamination
---------------------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Veterinary_public_health&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Environmental contamination")]

[Environmental pollutants](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Environmental_pollution "Environmental pollution") that arise through the keeping and use of animals may include [pollution](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pollution "Pollution") of the air, land or water. It can arise through [animal waste](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Animal_waste "Animal waste") products as well as [chemicals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chemical "Chemical") that may be used during production (e.g. [insecticides](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Insecticide "Insecticide"), [antibiotics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antibiotic "Antibiotic"), etc.).

In addition, practising vets will also produce potential environmental [contaminants](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Contaminant "Contaminant") in the form of used needles, syringes, animal tissue and other [clinical waste](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Clinical_waste "Clinical waste").

All of these materials have to be dealt with in a safe and controlled way. (See the section [Environmental Contamination](http://w01.rvcwiki.wf.ulcc.ac.uk/index.php?title=Introduction_to_VPH) on [WikiVet](http://www.wikivet.net/).)

Role of animals in society
--------------------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Veterinary_public_health&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Role of animals in society")]

This aspect of veterinary public health deals with a number of [ethical](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ethics "Ethics") issues. [Welfare of animals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Animal_welfare "Animal welfare") is an ever present issue regardless of the setting, whether it concerns pet animals, production animals or wild animals.

Where the line that defines acceptable and unacceptable welfare conditions lies is different for different individuals from different countries and cultures; however, it is common for minimum welfare standards to be defined in [legislation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Legislation "Legislation").[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Veterinary_public_health#cite_note-4)

Other issues may concern the use of animals in [science](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Science "Science"), not just for [experimentation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Animal_experimentation "Animal experimentation"), but the use of [transgenic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transgenic "Transgenic") animals (an animals who has had its [genome](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Genome "Genome") deliberately altered by [genetic engineering](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Genetic_engineering "Genetic engineering") techniques as opposed to [selective breeding](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Selective_breeding "Selective breeding")) and [xenotransplantation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Xenotransplantation "Xenotransplantation") (the transplantation of organs or tissues from one species to another) or the emergence of [resistance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Drug_resistance "Drug resistance") to [antimicrobial](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antimicrobial "Antimicrobial") drugs due to their use in animals.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Veterinary_public_health#cite_note-5)

References
----------

## Section navigation

# Veterinary public health

**Veterinary public health** is a component of public health that focuses on the application of veterinary science as a contribution to the protection and improvement of human well-being. Veterinary public health is applied to[neglected zoonotic diseases](/news-room/facts-in-pictures/detail/neglected-zoonotic-tropical-diseases), which are a subset of the neglected tropical diseases. Zoonoses are diseases naturally transmitted from vertebrate animals to humans and vice-versa.

Addressing this group of diseases requires collaborative, cross-sectoral efforts of human and animal health systems and a multidisciplinary approach that considers the complexities of the ecosystems where humans and animals coexist. Preventing and mitigating their occurrence in humans requires control and, where feasible, elimination of the diseases in their animal reservoirs. There is now recognition that several zoonotic diseases within the NTDs merit attention, inclusive of: rabies, dracunculiasis, echinococcosis, taeniasis/cysticercosis, foodborne trematodiases, human African trypanosomiasis, leishmaniasis and schistosomiasis.

Examples of veterinary public health interventions include:

![Every nine minutes, someone in low- and middle-income countries dies from rabies]( "3961_43372700_908_n")

Mission

### 

To reduce the burden of neglected zoonotic diseases on poor and marginalized populations in low-resource settings by advocating for strengthening their prevention and control through effective collaboration with strategic partners and relevant sectors.

Approaches

### 

Control of neglected zoonotic diseases calls for integrated interventions among human and animal health, and other relevant sectors. WHO approaches to reducing their impact on people’s health and livelihoods include:

Objectives

The WHO Department of Control of Neglected Tropical Diseases has identified priority neglected zoonotic diseases as rabies, taeniasis/cysticercosis, echinococcosis and foodborne trematodiases. Other areas of work will be considered as they emerge and based on need.

The Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO), the World Organisation for Animal Health (OIE) and the World Health Organization (WHO), known as the Tripartite, are working together to promote cross-sectoral collaboration to address risks from zoonoses and other public health threats that exist or emerge at the human-animal-ecosystems interface.

[The Regional Tripartite in Asia and the Pacific region, has jointly developed a series of resources to promote multisectoral collaboration](https://rr-asia.oie.int/en/projects/one-health/neglected-parasitic-zoonoses/awareness-materials-npz/) targeting public health practitioners, food safety and veterinary authorities, and other practitioners in Asia and the Pacific region, but the information is relevant in many other regions where these neglected zoonoses are present.

SUMMARY:
1. Veterinary public health is a component of public health that focuses on the application of veterinary science and that includes all actions

FULL CONTENT:
2022 © OIE - Terrestrial Animal Health Code - 10/08/2022 1 S E C T I O N 6 .
V E T E R I N A R Y P U B L I C H E A L T H C H A P T E R 6 . 1 .
I N T R O D U C T I O N T O R E C O M M E N D A T I O N S F O R V E T E R I N A R Y P U B L I C H E A L T H Article 6.1.1.
Veterinary public health is a component of public health that focuses on the application of veterinary science and that includes all actions directly or indirectly linked with animals, their products and by-products, so long as they contribute to the protection and improvement of the physical, mental and social well-being of humans.
Veterinary science has a rich history of contributions to public health, especially with regard to the provision of safe and adequate food, the prevention, control and eradication of zoonoses, the improvement of animal welfare and contributing to biomedical research.
Veterinary Services play a key role in preventing, mitigating and controlling risks to public health at the origin or source of infection. In particular, Veterinary Services contribute to public health in several areas such as food security, food safety (with respect to foodborne diseases as well as residues and pollutants), control of zoonoses and responses to natural disasters and bioterrorism.
Furthermore, a number of anthropogenic factors influence the occurrence of emerging diseases, some of which are zoonotic. These factors include among others: population growth and changing food consumption patterns and their consequences such as increasing food demand and intensification of production systems; increased movements and trade of animals and their products and derived products; the use and misuse of antimicrobial agents generating resistance; the disruption of ecosystems; climate change.
In this context, Veterinary Services are integrated into the ’One Health’ approach to the assessment, prevention, management and communication of health risks and preservation of the integrity of ecosystems for the benefit of human health, the health of domestic animals and wildlife, and biodiversity.
Veterinary training and education should take into account the role of Veterinary Services in public health at national, regional and global level in the development of veterinary public health capabilities.
NB: FIRST ADOPTED IN 2018.
2 2022 © OIE - Terrestrial Animal Health Code - 10/08/2022 Chapter 6.1.- Introduction to recommendations for veterinary public health

Veterinary public health
========================

PMCID: PMC1289303 PMID: [16308356](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16308356/)

* * *

**T**he World Health Organization defines veterinary public health as, “The sum of all contributions to the complete physical, mental and social well-being of humans through an understanding and application of veterinary medical science.”[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1289303/#ref1) With this definition every veterinary surgeon contributes to public health, whether through provision of health care for pets, protection of animal welfare, biomedical research, or ensuring adequate food animal production and food safety.

Veterinary public health impacts on human health by reducing exposure to hazards arising from animals, animal products, and their environment. Examples of these hazards include zoonoses, vector borne infections and other communicable diseases, chemicals and drugs used in animals, envenomations, and injuries from exposure to animals.[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1289303/#ref1)

The concept of veterinary public health originates in ancient Egypt, when healer priests drew no distinctions between caring for human patients and animals. They gained much knowledge from the anatomy and diseases of animals, which they applied to the healing of humans.[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1289303/#ref2) This “one medicine” approach prevailed until the 19th century. Since then, the gulf between human and animal physicians has been increasing, mainly because of changes in political and cultural rules rather than scientific logic.[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1289303/#ref2) Although public and political interest in veterinary public health declined in Britain towards the end of the 20th century, it remained an important part of veterinary education and is now a recognised specialty in the United Kingdom and the rest of Europe.[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1289303/#ref3)

Newly emerging and re-emerging infections are recognised as a global problem, and 75% of these are potentially zoonotic.[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1289303/#ref4) The general public and health professionals perceive that the emergence of a new “killer” disease in any area of the world is a threat for all humans.[5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1289303/#ref5) Recent examples that support this belief include the outbreaks of H5N1 avian influenza in Asia, bovine spongiform encephalopathy in the United Kingdom, West Nile virus and monkeypox in North America, and H7N7 avian influenza in the Netherlands.[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1289303/#ref6)

The re-emergence of zoonoses, together with other issues such as bioterrorism, pollution incidents, antimicrobial resistance, xenotransplantation, and the socioeconomic importance of food production, make a collaborative interprofessional approach to veterinary public health more urgent. The specialty may also offer early insights into the cause of unusual or unexplained illness.[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1289303/#ref7) Multidisciplinary teams comprising all those who contribute to the treatment, control, and prevention of diseases of animal origin are essential, not only to determine the source of disease but also to assess the risk of further outbreaks and to make recommendations for future controls.[8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1289303/#ref8),[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1289303/#ref9)

Figure 1.
---------

[![Image 9: Figure 1](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/b14a/1289303/e464dd1acdc8/edit1205.f6.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=1289303_edit1205.f6.jpg)

Raptor keepers at the Woodland Park Zoo in Seattle give Otus, a Western Screech Owl, a vaccination for the West Nile virus

Credit: LOREN CALLAGHAN/AP

Likewise, liaison between organisations at government and national levels is a prerequisite for deciding policies and disseminating information clearly and quickly. Local collaboration also has an important role in discussing local and national issues. Meetings are currently mostly confined to regular and ad hoc meetings between professionals from organisations such as the state veterinary service, health protection agencies, and local and water authorities. [10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1289303/#ref10) Little routine local collaboration occurs between the medical and veterinary professions in general practice.

But sharing local knowledge and expertise would have real benefits for the immediate human and animal populations. Sporadic or community and animal outbreaks of salmonellosis may, for example, have a common source of which only one profession is aware. Other potential benefits include reduced incidence of antibiotic resistance through sharing and comparing use, highlighting common environmental hazards such as farm discharges, and identifying research priorities.

The time has surely come for medical and veterinary general practitioners to get together to share knowledge and voice concerns. Local concerns, which may become national problems, can be identified only through an open and integrated professional approach. Willingness by both the veterinary and medical professions at both local and national levels to collaborate and share information is therefore essential for the protection and promotion of public health.

Competing interests: None declared.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Veterinary public health integrates veterinary science and public health, focusing on protecting human well-being through animal health, food safety, and the management of zoonotic diseases.

enriched_points.point_title: Importance of Animal Health in Human Health
enriched_points.expanded_content: Animal health directly influences human health by mitigating risks from zoonoses, ensuring food safety, and fostering ecological balance, thereby enhancing overall public health outcomes.
Zoonotic diseases, transmitted between animals and humans, pose significant public health threats, necessitating coordinated efforts to control and prevent their emergence and spread.
